seasonal_wine.name: Albariza vine flowering: May
seasonal_wine.description: Palomino Fino flowers in May across the albariza chalk vineyards of the Jerez Superior zone. Cool Atlantic breezes from the Cadiz coast moderate temperatures and extend the flowering window. Viticulturalists monitor the flower set closely; Jerez's chalky soils retain March and April rainfall at depth to sustain the vines through the approaching summer heat.
seasonal_wine.evidence: The Consejo Regulador and Sherry Wine Council publish the annual viticultural calendar on their websites.

seasonal_wine.name: Veraison and summer ripening: July-August
seasonal_wine.description: The Jerez summer is extreme: temperatures regularly exceed 40 degrees Celsius and rainfall is near zero from June through September. Albariza chalk acts as a water reservoir released slowly to the vine roots, making the soils uniquely suited to sustain Palomino Fino through the heat. Levante winds from North Africa can spike temperatures for days at a time, accelerating sugar accumulation and reinforcing the need for early harvest timing.
seasonal_wine.evidence: Climatic data and viticultural calendar published by the Consejo Regulador and documented on the Sherry Wine Council geography pages.

seasonal_wine.name: Vendimia: harvest season, early-mid September
seasonal_wine.description: The Jerez harvest, or vendimia, typically begins in the first week of September, earlier than most European wine regions due to Andalucia's intense summer heat. Palomino Fino is harvested at around 11-11.5 degrees Baume for the fino and manzanilla base wines; Pedro Ximenez grapes are left on the vine longer or harvested first for the asoleo (sun-drying) process. Traditionally the first grapes were pressed by venenciadors in a ceremonial treading on the steps of the Collegiate Church of Jerez.
seasonal_wine.evidence: Harvest timing and viticultural practices are confirmed by the Consejo Regulador and documented by the Sherry Wine Council's production pages.

seasonal_wine.name: Asoleo: Pedro Ximenez sun-drying on esparto mats, September
seasonal_wine.description: Immediately after harvest, Pedro Ximenez and Moscatel grapes destined for sweet sherry are spread on circular esparto grass mats on the open ground beside the bodega and left in the September sun for 10-14 days. The process, called asoleo, concentrates sugars to extraordinary levels, turning the berries into near-raisins before pressing. The result is a must with natural sugar levels above 400 grams per litre. Only a handful of bodegas still carry out asoleo on their own grapes.
seasonal_wine.evidence: The asoleo process and its role in Pedro Ximenez production are confirmed by the Consejo Regulador and the Sherry Wine Council.

seasonal_wine.name: First fermentation and classification of new must, October
seasonal_wine.description: The freshly pressed must ferments to dryness in stainless steel tanks or American oak butts over approximately two to three weeks in October. At the end of fermentation, cellar masters taste and classify every new wine: lighter, more delicate casks are designated for fino or manzanilla (biological ageing under flor); fuller, more robust casks are designated for oloroso (oxidative ageing). This classification is the critical decision point that determines a wine's entire subsequent trajectory.
seasonal_wine.evidence: The classification process after first fermentation is described in detail by the Consejo Regulador and the Sherry Wine Council's how-sherry-is-made pages.

seasonal_wine.name: Flor growth season: peak activity, October-May
seasonal_wine.description: The flor yeast veil that protects fino and manzanilla from oxidation grows most actively at temperatures between 15 and 20 degrees Celsius, making the cooler Andalucian autumn and spring the peak biological ageing windows. In Sanlucar de Barrameda, the Atlantic humidity sustains the flor veil throughout the year at greater thickness than in Jerez, which is why manzanilla develops its distinctive lighter, saltier, chamomile-like character. During the hot summer months flor thins and the wines can shift toward the amontillado style.
seasonal_wine.evidence: Flor biology and its seasonal rhythms are described in detail by the Consejo Regulador and by the Oxford Companion to Wine.

verified.address_quoted: Jerez de la Frontera, Sanlucar de Barrameda

seasonal_wine.name: En rama spring bottling: April-May
seasonal_wine.description: Most Jerez and Sanlucar bodegas release their en rama editions in late April and May when the flor veil is at its thickest and most active following winter dormancy. These minimally-filtered bottlings, drawn directly from the criaderas (nursery barrels) of the solera, capture the freshest, most complex expression of fino and manzanilla before summer heat thins the flor. Collectors track release dates from Gonzalez Byass, Lustau, Hidalgo La Gitana, and Barbadillo each spring.
seasonal_wine.evidence: Spring en rama bottling windows are confirmed by individual producer release calendars and documented by multiple wine journalists covering the Jerez revival.

seasonal_wine.name: Sacas and rocio: solera transfers, year-round
seasonal_wine.description: The solera system involves periodic sacas (withdrawals) from the oldest barrels and rocio (irrigation) additions of younger wine to replenish them, typically two to three times per year. No more than one-third of any barrel may be withdrawn at each saca to preserve the character of the old wine. The timing of sacas affects the flavour profile: winter sacas from fino soleras produce wines with the highest flor activity and freshest character; summer sacas from oloroso soleras release the most concentrated oxidative expressions.
seasonal_wine.evidence: The solera saca and rocio process is described in detail by the Consejo Regulador and by the Sherry Wine Council.

seasonal_wine.name: Feria del Caballo: the May sherry social season
seasonal_wine.description: The Feria del Caballo (Horse Fair) held each May in Jerez is the year's most socially significant sherry consumption event. Thousands of decorated horse-drawn carriages parade through the fairground while attendees in flamenco dress drink chilled fino and manzanilla from traditional long-stemmed copitas in the casetas (tent pavilions). For the city's bodegas, the Feria represents the highest-volume selling week of the year for local fino.
seasonal_wine.evidence: The Feria del Caballo and its role in sherry culture are documented by the City of Jerez and the Sherry Wine Council.

seasonal_wine.name: Sanlucar beach horse races and Manzanilla: August
seasonal_wine.description: The Carreras de Caballos en la Playa de Sanlucar de Barrameda, held on the beach at Sanlucar de Barrameda each August since 1845, are among Europe's oldest beach horse races and the most photogenic event in the Sherry Triangle's wine-tourism calendar. Spectators line the beach drinking chilled Manzanilla from the town's tabancos and bodegas while the horses race on the wet sand at the mouth of the Guadalquivir. Hidalgo La Gitana and Barbadillo are the dominant Manzanilla brands sold at the races.
seasonal_wine.evidence: The Sanlucar beach races have been held since 1845 as confirmed by multiple historical sources and the Junta de Andalucia tourism authority.

seasonal_wine.name: Fiesta de la Vendimia de Jerez: ceremonial harvest festival, September
seasonal_wine.description: The Fiesta de la Vendimia de Jerez, held on the first Sunday in September, is the traditional public opening of the Jerez harvest. The ceremony, held on the steps of the Collegiate Church of San Salvador, features the treading of the first grapes by foot in traditional Jerez esparto baskets, a blessing of the must by the Archbishop of Jerez, and the first ceremonial filling of the venencia, the long-handled sherry sampling cup. The event draws pilgrims from across the wine world to witness the opening of the harvest season.
seasonal_wine.evidence: The Fiesta de la Vendimia de Jerez is described by the Consejo Regulador and the Sherry Wine Council as the opening ritual of the harvest season.

seasonal_wine.name: Christmas and festive season: the oloroso and PX moment, December
seasonal_wine.description: Oloroso, Cream, and Pedro Ximenez sherries reach their seasonal peak of sales in December, when the Spanish tradition of serving sweet sherry with turrón and polvorones (Andalucian Christmas biscuits) drives the highest volume of domestic consumption. The festive period is also when Spain's supermarkets and wine shops stock their widest sherry selections, and the grandes marcas (Gonzalez Byass, Lustau, Osborne, Williams and Humbert) launch their Christmas gift editions. Palo cortado, valued for its combination of amontillado aroma and oloroso body, has grown rapidly as a prestige Christmas gift.
seasonal_wine.evidence: The seasonal peak of oloroso and PX consumption in Spain at Christmas is documented by the Consejo Regulador's production and sales statistics.
